7 min read
2 hours ago

--

When we build backend systems, we usually imagine the happy path.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A user sends a request.
Our server receives it.
We process it.
We store some data.
We return a response.
Everything looks simple.
But real-world systems are not always that smooth.
Sometimes traffic suddenly increases. Sometimes the database becomes slow. Sometimes your cache gets full. Sometimes your worker is not able to process events as fast as they are coming in.
That moment is where backpressure becomes important.
Backpressure is not a fancy concept. It simply means:
“The system is receiving more work than it can safely handle, so it needs a smart way to slow down, reject, drop, or control extra work.”
In simple words, backpressure is how your system protects itself from overload.
A Simple Real-Life Example
Imagine you run a small tea shop.
Normally, 5 people come every 10 minutes. You can easily prepare tea for everyone.
But suddenly, 100 people come at the same time.
Now you have a few choices:
You can try to serve everyone at once and create complete chaos.
You can ask people to wait in a queue.
You can stop taking new orders for some time.
You can serve only limited items.
Or you can say, “Sorry, we are full right now.”
That is backpressure in real life.
Your shop has limited capacity. If you ignore that limit, everything breaks.
Software systems behave in the same way.
Backpressure in Backend Systems
In backend systems, backpressure happens when one part of the system is producing work faster than another part can consume it.
For example:
Your API receives 10,000 requests per second, but your database can only handle 2,000 writes per second.
Your application keeps adding events into a queue, but your worker is too slow to process them.
Your in-memory cache keeps receiving new data, but memory is limited.
Your analytics system cannot record every click during a traffic spike.
In all these cases, if you do not apply backpressure, your system may become slow, crash, or start failing badly.
Backpressure in Caching
Let’s take an example of an in-memory cache.
Suppose you are storing frequently accessed data in memory because memory is fast. But memory is not unlimited.
At some point, your cache may become full.
Now what should your system do?
Should it keep adding more data until the server crashes?
Obviously not.
This is where backpressure strategies come in.
When the cache is full, the system can:
Remove old entries.
Use TTL to expire data automatically.
Reject new cache writes.
Evict less useful data.
Serve the request without caching the result.
Reduce the amount of data being stored.
The main goal is simple:
“Do not let the cache overload bring down the whole system.”
Example: Link Shortener Click Recorder
Let’s understand this with a practical example.
Suppose you are building a link shortener.
When a user clicks a short link, two things can happen:
First, the user should be redirected to the original URL.
Second, we may want to record analytics like click count, location, device, browser, and timestamp.
But here is the important point:
The redirect is critical.
Analytics are useful, but not more important than the redirect.
So we can divide the system into two paths:
Hot Path
The hot path is the most important and time-sensitive path.
In a link shortener, the hot path is:
User clicks short link → server finds original URL → user gets redirected.
This should be extremely fast.
Cold Path
The cold path is less urgent.
In our case, click analytics can be handled in the background.
User clicks short link → event is sent to a queue or channel → worker processes it later → analytics are saved to database.
This design keeps the redirect fast.
Where Backpressure Comes In
Now imagine your link shortener suddenly gets a lot of traffic.
Users are clicking links very fast.
Your server is redirecting users properly, but your analytics worker is not able to process click events fast enough.
The queue starts filling.
The in-memory buffer starts filling.
The cache or channel reaches its limit.
Now your system has a decision to make.
Should it block the user redirect just because analytics are not being recorded fast enough?
No.
That would be a bad user experience.
Instead, we can apply backpressure.
For example:
If the analytics queue is full, we can drop some click events.
If traffic is too high, we can sample events, like record only 1 out of every 10 clicks.
If the database is slow, we can keep redirecting users and temporarily skip analytics.
If the cache is full, we can serve the request without caching new data.
This is called graceful degradation.
The system does not completely fail. It only reduces non-critical work.
Backpressure vs Graceful Degradation
Backpressure and graceful degradation are connected, but they are not exactly the same.
Backpressure is the overall technique of controlling overload.
Graceful degradation means reducing some features instead of breaking the whole system.
In our link shortener example:
If the analytics queue is full and we stop recording some clicks, that is load shedding.
If we still redirect users successfully, that is graceful degradation.
If we use this strategy to protect the system from overload, it becomes part of backpressure handling.
So we can say:
“Backpressure helps the system survive overload, and graceful degradation helps the user still get the most important experience.”
Does TTL Remove the Need for Backpressure?
TTL is very useful in caching.
TTL means Time To Live.
It automatically removes cache entries after a fixed time.
For example, if we cache a URL mapping for 10 minutes, it will be removed automatically after 10 minutes.
This helps reduce memory usage.
But TTL does not completely remove the need for backpressure.
Why?
Because traffic spikes can happen before old cache entries expire.
For example, suppose your cache entries expire after 10 minutes, but suddenly 1 million new links are accessed within 30 seconds.
Your cache can still fill up quickly.
So TTL helps with memory management, but it does not fully solve overload.
A better statement would be:
“TTL reduces memory pressure, but backpressure is still needed when traffic spikes or when producers are faster than consumers.”
Common Backpressure Strategies
There are many ways to handle backpressure.
1. Queueing
You can put extra work into a queue and process it later.
This is useful when the work does not need to happen immediately.
Example:
Store click events in a queue and process them in the background.
But queues should also have limits. An unlimited queue can also crash your system.
2. Dropping Non-Critical Events
Sometimes it is okay to drop less important data.
For example, in a link shortener, redirecting the user is more important than recording every single analytics event.
So if the analytics buffer is full, we can drop the event.
This is better than slowing down the redirect.
3. Sampling
Instead of recording every event, we record only some events.
For example:
Record 1 out of every 10 clicks.
This still gives useful analytics without overloading the system.
Sampling is common in logging, monitoring, and analytics systems.
4. Rate Limiting
Rate limiting means allowing only a fixed number of requests in a given time.
For example:
A user can make only 100 requests per minute.
This protects your system from abuse and sudden overload.
5. Cache Eviction
When cache is full, we can remove old or less useful data.
Common eviction strategies include:
LRU: Remove least recently used data.
LFU: Remove least frequently used data.
FIFO: Remove the oldest data first.
This helps the cache stay within memory limits.
6. Rejecting Requests
In some cases, the system can directly reject extra requests.
For example:
“The server is busy. Please try again later.”
This is better than accepting all requests and crashing completely.
Why Backpressure Matters
Backpressure matters because every system has limits.
Your server has limited CPU.
Your database has limited write capacity.
Your cache has limited memory.
Your workers have limited processing power.
Ignoring these limits is dangerous.
A good system does not assume infinite capacity.
A good system asks:
What happens when the queue is full?
What happens when Redis is slow?
What happens when the database is overloaded?
What happens when traffic suddenly becomes 10x?
What should we drop?
What should we protect?
What should never be blocked?
These questions make your system more production-ready.
Final Thought
Backpressure is not about making your system perfect.
It is about making your system safe under pressure.
In a link shortener, the most important thing is redirecting the user quickly. Analytics are useful, but they should not block the redirect.
So a good design would be:
Redirect users immediately.
Send analytics to a background worker.
Use a limited queue or buffer.
Apply TTL and cache eviction.
If the buffer is full, drop or sample analytics events.
Protect the hot path at all costs.
That is how real systems are designed.
Because in production, the question is not only:
“Can my system work?”
The real question is:
“Can my system still behave properly when things go wrong?”
